intro: Calculate your selling price instantly from cost and markup percentage. Know exactly how much profit you make on every sale and what gross margin you're operating at.
howItWorks: Enter your cost price (what you pay) and the markup percentage you want to apply. The calculator computes: Selling Price = Cost × (1 + Markup/100), Gross Profit = Selling Price − Cost, and Gross Margin = Gross Profit ÷ Selling Price × 100. Markup and gross margin are related but different: a 50% markup on a £10 cost gives a £15 selling price and a 33.3% gross margin.
interpretationGuide: Markup is calculated on cost; gross margin is calculated on selling price. A 100% markup doubles the price but gives only a 50% margin. Industries typically target margins of 30–70% for products, 50–80% for services. If your margin is too thin, raise the markup or reduce cost. Use this calculator alongside your break-even calculator to ensure you cover fixed costs.

faqs.question: What is the difference between markup and margin?
faqs.answer: Markup is calculated from cost: a 50% markup on £10 cost = £15 selling price. Gross margin is calculated from the selling price: £5 profit ÷ £15 = 33.3% margin. Markup is always a larger number than the resulting margin.

faqs.question: How do I convert markup to margin?
faqs.answer: Margin = Markup ÷ (1 + Markup). For a 50% markup: 0.5 ÷ 1.5 = 33.3% margin. To go the other way, Markup = Margin ÷ (1 − Margin).

faqs.question: What markup should I use for retail products?
faqs.answer: Retail typically uses 40–100% markup (28–50% margin). Grocery runs 25–50% markup. Electronics 10–30%. Clothing 100–300%. Services often aim for 100–300% markup on direct costs.

faqs.question: What is a good gross margin for a small business?
faqs.answer: A gross margin of 40–60% is considered healthy for most product businesses. Software and SaaS companies often achieve 70–90%. Service businesses typically target 50–70%. Gross margin must be high enough to cover operating expenses and still produce profit.

faqs.question: How do I price a product to achieve a target margin?
faqs.answer: Use the formula: Selling Price = Cost ÷ (1 − Target Margin). To hit a 40% margin on a £20 cost: £20 ÷ 0.6 = £33.33. Note this is different from applying a 40% markup, which would give £28.

faqs.question: Does markup include VAT?
faqs.answer: No — markup and margin calculations are on the ex-VAT (net) price. VAT is added on top and collected on behalf of the government. When pricing for consumers, add VAT to your calculated selling price.

faqs.question: Can markup be over 100%?
faqs.answer: Yes. A 200% markup on a £10 item gives a £30 selling price, which is a 66.7% gross margin. Luxury goods, specialty items, and services often carry markups of 200–500%.

faqs.question: What is cost price?
faqs.answer: Cost price is what you pay to acquire or produce one unit of a product — including materials, labour, and direct manufacturing costs. It does not include overheads like rent, marketing, or salaries unless you are doing a full-cost pricing model.

faqs.question: How do I account for discounts in my markup?
faqs.answer: Build discounts into your pricing strategy. If you plan to offer 20% discounts regularly, set your base markup high enough that you still hit your margin target after the discount. Use the invoice calculator to check the net amount after discounting.

faqs.question: Should I use cost-plus or value-based pricing?
faqs.answer: Cost-plus pricing (markup on cost) ensures profitability but can leave money on the table. Value-based pricing sets the price based on what customers will pay. In practice, check both: use cost-plus to find your floor price and value-based thinking to determine how high you can go.

faqs.question: What markup do I need for a 50% gross margin?
faqs.answer: To achieve a 50% gross margin, you need a 100% markup. Formula: Markup = Margin ÷ (1 − Margin) = 0.5 ÷ 0.5 = 1.0 = 100%.

faqs.question: Are markup calculations different for services vs products?
faqs.answer: The formula is the same, but 'cost' for services typically means direct labour and materials. Many service businesses use a multiplier on direct labour cost — for example, 3× labour rate — which equates to a 200% markup and a 66.7% gross margin.
